did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes an automatic algorithm for classifying ridge and furrow patterns in dermoscopic images to distinguish acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) from nevi. The method involves image analysis techniques such as Gaussian derivative filtering for edge detection, measuring stripe widths, tagging brightness, and calculating width ratios to generate histograms for classification. While the abstract mentions 'automatic algorithm' and the introduction references artificial intelligence in the context of tele-dermatology, the specific method described is a rule-based image processing and pattern classification approach rather than a machine learning or deep learning model.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'Original article' in the header. It presents a novel automatic algorithm for classifying ridge and furrow patterns in dermoscopic images, detailing specific methods (Gaussian derivative filtering, width ratio calculation), results from a dataset of 297 images, and performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy). This structure and content are characteristic of an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: Ridge and furrow pattern classification for acral lentiginous melanoma using dermoscopic images
brief_summary: This study developed an automatic algorithm to distinguish acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) from nevi by classifying ridge and furrow pigmentation patterns in dermoscopic images. The method utilized Gaussian derivative filtering to detect edges, measured stripe widths, and calculated the ratio of dark to bright areas to generate a width ratio histogram. The algorithm was evaluated on a dataset of 297 dermoscopic images confirmed by histopathologic diagnosis. The proposed method achieved a sensitivity of 100%, a specificity of 99.1%, and an accuracy of 99.7%. All melanoma images were correctly classified, with only one nevus image misclassified. The results suggest the algorithm can precisely distinguish pigmentation patterns and contribute to the early diagnosis of ALM.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the classification algorithm. The study focuses on distinguishing ALM (specifically those with ridge patterns) from nevi. All melanoma images in the dataset were correctly classified.
The comparator class used to distinguish from ALM. One nevus image was misclassified in the study.

all_task_types.target: Distinguishing between acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) and nevus based on ridge and furrow pigmentation patterns.
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task. The algorithm classifies dermoscopic images into two discrete diagnostic categories: ALM (ridge pattern) or nevus (furrow pattern). The study reports sensitivity (100%), specificity (99.1%), and accuracy (99.7%) for this classification.

datasets_used.name: Histopathologically confirmed dermoscopic image dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermoscopic images of acral skin lesions confirmed by histopathologic diagnosis, used to evaluate the proposed automatic algorithm for distinguishing ridge and furrow patterns.

use.testing: The dataset was used to evaluate the performance of the proposed algorithm, reporting sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and similarity metrics on a dataset of 297 images.

Histopathologically confirmed dermoscopi.notes: All melanoma dermoscopic images were classified correctly. Only one nevus image was misclassified.

architecture_details.method_type: Rule-based image processing and pattern classification algorithm
architecture_details.description: An automatic algorithm for classifying ridge and furrow patterns in dermoscopic images. It does not use a neural network or deep learning model family.
steps: Edge detection using Gaussian derivative filtering to find edges with the steepest ascent or descent.
Measurement of widths between detected edges.
Tagging brightness of each stripe (dark area as black, bright area as white).
Calculation of the ratio of widths of dark to bright areas at each stripe pair.
Generation of a histogram of the width ratio in the dermoscopic image.

classification_or_output_head.details: Classification is performed based on the generated histogram of the width ratio of dark and bright patterns to distinguish between 'ridge' and 'furrow' patterns.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed automatic algorithm (rule-based image processing and pattern classification using Gaussian derivative filtering, stripe width measurement, brightness tagging, and width ratio histogram generation)
preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian derivative filtering for edge detection
Tagging brightness of stripes (dark area as black, bright area as white)
conclusions: The study proposes a novel automatic algorithm that precisely distinguishes between "furrow" and "ridge" pigmentation patterns in dermoscopic images by utilizing the width ratio of dark and bright patterns.
The proposed algorithm achieved a sensitivity of 100%, a specificity of 99.1%, an accuracy of 99.7%, and a similarity of 99.7% when classifying 297 histopathologically confirmed dermoscopic images.
All melanoma dermoscopic images were correctly classified, with only one nevus image misclassified.
The proposed algorithm is expected to contribute to the early diagnosis of acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM).
important_remarks: The study addresses a specific clinical challenge: ALM is the most common melanoma in non-Caucasian populations, but automatic diagnosis is difficult due to its distinct appearance compared to melanoma in other sites.
The algorithm focuses specifically on the 'ridge' pattern, which is the most frequent pattern for ALM, rather than covering all ALM patterns (furrow, parallel, lattice).
The dataset consists of 297 histopathologically confirmed dermoscopic images, providing a solid ground truth basis for the evaluation.

The reported performance is exceptionally high (100% sensitivity, 99.1% specificity, 99.7% accuracy), with only one nevus misclassified; this suggests potential overfitting or a lack of external validation on diverse, unseen populations.

The method is rule-based (Gaussian derivative filtering, width ratio histogram) rather than a machine learning model, which limits its ability to learn complex, non-linear features automatically but may offer interpretability.

No external validation dataset is mentioned; the evaluation is confined to the single internal dataset of 297 images, raising concerns about generalizability to other clinical settings or image acquisition devices.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical robustness of the results.

The study highlights the difficulty of applying automated diagnosis to darker-pigmented individuals, where ALM is prevalent, implying that the algorithm's performance might vary across different skin phototypes if not specifically trained on them.
